preamble: The diphthong /ie/ can undergo a transformation to the nasalized close-mid front unrounded vowel /ɛ̃/ through a process involving both monophthongization and nasalization. This shift is notably observed in the historical development of French, where oral diphthongs in certain contexts evolved into nasalized monophthongs.
phoneticEffects: Monophthongization, Nasalization, Vowel Lowering (from /i/ to /ɛ/ within the diphthong's realization)

examples.note: The Latin short /e/ in 'bene' diphthongized to /ie/ in Old French. Subsequently, this diphthong became nasalized due to a following nasal consonant, and then underwent monophthongization and lowering, resulting in the modern French nasalized vowel /ɛ̃/ (often with a preceding /j/ glide).

sources: Modeling the emergence of a typological anomaly: Vowel nasalization in French. Kennedy Terry, K., & Russell, E. (n.d.). Proceedings.
How and why did French add "i"s behind "en" in some of its words (ex: bene=>bien)?. (2025, January 26). Quora.
